items.q: Did the Berlin Blockade lead to direct fighting between the superpowers?
items.correct: No - the Western Allies avoided armed confrontation by flying supplies into West Berlin for almost a year, until Stalin lifted the blockade in May 1949.
items.misconception: The Berlin Blockade ended when Allied troops fought their way into Berlin.
items.random_wrong: The Berlin Blockade was a naval blockade of Germany's ports.
items.why: Students assume Cold War crises ended in battles; Berlin shows the superpowers confronting each other through pressure and resources, not direct combat.

items.q: Why was the Berlin Wall built in 1961?
items.correct: East Germany built it to stop the flood of its own citizens escaping to the West through West Berlin, which was draining skilled workers.
items.misconception: The Berlin Wall was built to keep Western invaders out of East Berlin.
items.random_wrong: The Berlin Wall was built by the Americans to protect West Berlin.
items.why: East German propaganda called it an 'anti-fascist protection barrier', but its real purpose was to stop emigration, which students often reverse.

items.q: Did the Cold War officially end the night the Berlin Wall fell in 1989?
items.correct: No - the Wall's fall was symbolic, but the Cold War ended through a process running to the dissolution of the USSR in December 1991.
items.misconception: The Cold War ended on the night of 9 November 1989 when the Wall came down.
items.random_wrong: The Cold War ended when Germany joined the United Nations.
items.why: Dramatic moments get remembered as endpoints; in fact communist regimes collapsed over 1989-91 and the USSR itself only broke up in 1991.

items.q: What did the Truman Doctrine of 1947 commit the USA to?
items.correct: Containing the spread of communism by supporting countries under threat
items.why: Correct answer: Containing the spread of communism by supporting countries under threat. “Invading the Soviet Union” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: How did the Western Allies respond to the Berlin Blockade of 1948-49?
items.correct: They supplied West Berlin by air for almost a year until the blockade was lifted
items.misconception: They sent tanks through the Soviet checkpoints
items.random_wrong: They abandoned West Berlin to Soviet control
items.why: The answer examiners want is: They supplied West Berlin by air for almost a year until the blockade was lifted. “They sent tanks through the Soviet checkpoints” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: How was the Cuban Missile Crisis of 1962 resolved?
items.correct: Khrushchev withdrew the missiles in return for a US pledge not to invade Cuba and the secret removal of US missiles from Turkey
items.misconception: The USA invaded Cuba and removed the missiles by force
items.random_wrong: The United Nations took over the government of Cuba
items.why: Correct answer: Khrushchev withdrew the missiles in return for a US pledge not to invade Cuba and the secret removal of US missiles from Turkey. “The USA invaded Cuba and removed the missiles by force” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Which event in November 1989 became the symbol of the end of the Cold War in Europe?
items.correct: The fall of the Berlin Wall
items.why: Correct answer: The fall of the Berlin Wall. “The Cuban Missile Crisis” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: In what year did the Berlin Wall fall?
items.why: The real figure is 1989 AD. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
